surface.name: Developer quick start, Docker and Go path
surface.selection_basis: HashiCorp labels this page Developer quick start and explicitly defines its goal as using a Vault client library in application code to store and retrieve a first secret. Docker is the first server-start option and Go is the first client-library tab, so this record uses those documented defaults while preserving the local-binary and other-language variants as branches.

alternatives_considered.name: Developer quick start with local Vault binary
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is the second server-start option and adds platform-specific Vault installation.

alternatives_considered.name: Vault CLI foundations
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It teaches CLI and server administration actions rather than the application-code milestone named by the developer quick start.

alternatives_considered.name: HCP Vault Dedicated
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a managed-cluster setup path with account, billing or trial, cluster, networking, and namespace gates.

entry_point.developer_intent: Use Vault from application code to store and retrieve a first secret value.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has not started a Vault server or configured a Vault client.
The Docker and Go variants are used because they are the first documented options in the selected quick start.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Write and retrieve your first Vault secret.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: A local Go program stores the password value in Vault's version 2 key-value engine, retrieves it, confirms the value, and prints Access granted!.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The final program run prints Access granted! after the retrieved password equals Hashi123.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: That's it! You've just written and retrieved your first Vault secret!

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: The guide states this as its completed first-secret outcome immediately after the retrieval check. The following Additional examples section introduces stronger authentication, runnable demos, dynamic database credentials, and Vault Agent.

prerequisites.requirement: A current Docker installation, used here instead of a local Vault binary.
A Go development environment and a Go source file or module in which to add the quickstart code.
Local TCP port 8200 must be available for the Docker container's published Vault listener.

primary_path.action: Open the Vault Developer quick start and select the documented Docker and Go variants.
details: The guide offers Docker or a local Vault binary for the server and Go, Ruby, C#, Python, Java, Bash/curl, and beta OpenAPI variants for the client.
This record follows Docker and the first code tab, Go.
primary_path.input: Intent to use Vault in application code.
primary_path.output: A concrete local-server and language path.
primary_path.success_signal: Docker and Go instructions are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Choosing another variant changes installation, code, and some terminal output.

primary_path.action: Install or confirm current Docker and a working Go development environment.
details: The quick start accepts Docker or a local Vault installation and requires a development environment for one supported language.
The Docker note warns that older versions may not work.
primary_path.success_signal: Docker and Go can execute locally.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Installation is operating-system specific and is linked rather than decomposed in the quick start.

primary_path.action: Start an insecure, in-memory Vault development server in Docker.
details: Run docker run -p 8200:8200 -e 'VAULT_DEV_ROOT_TOKEN_ID=dev-only-token' hashicorp/vault.
The token grants full root access and is only for this local development exercise.
The server listens over HTTP on local port 8200.
primary_path.output: Running Vault dev server with root token dev-only-token.
primary_path.success_signal: The container starts and Vault listens on port 8200.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the image is not local, Docker must download it. A port conflict or failed container prevents the next steps.

primary_path.action: Install the official Go Vault API client.
details: Run go get github.com/hashicorp/vault/api in the Go project.
primary_path.input: Go module and network access to the module registry.
primary_path.output: Vault Go API dependency added to the project.
primary_path.success_signal: The dependency installation completes without error.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Module, network, or dependency-resolution errors must be corrected before compiling.

primary_path.action: Import the Vault Go API package.
details: Add import vault "github.com/hashicorp/vault/api" to the source file.
The complete sample also needs standard-library context, fmt, and log imports for later steps.
primary_path.output: Source file can reference the Vault client library.
primary_path.success_signal: The import resolves in the Go project.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A missing module or malformed import causes compilation failure.

primary_path.action: Create a Vault client for the local address and set the development root token.
details: Create the default config and set its Address to http://127.0.0.1:8200.
Create the client and fail if initialization returns an error.
Call client.SetToken("dev-only-token").
The guide notes that production should use a more secure authentication method.
primary_path.input: Local Vault address and dev-only root token.
primary_path.success_signal: Client initialization completes and carries the token for requests.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An incorrect address or token causes the later write request to fail.

primary_path.action: Add code that stores the first key-value secret.
details: Create a map whose password key has value Hashi123.
Call client.KVv2("secret").Put with path my-secret-password and the map.
Handle the returned error and print Secret written successfully.
primary_path.input: Authenticated Vault client, secret mount secret, path my-secret-password, and password value.
primary_path.output: Go program containing the KV v2 write request.
primary_path.success_signal: The program compiles with the write action.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Incorrect code, mount, address, or token surfaces as a compile or request error.

primary_path.action: Run the program and verify that the secret write succeeds.
details: The guide instructs the developer to run the code after adding the write.
If it succeeds, the program prints Secret written successfully.
primary_path.input: Running Vault dev server and completed write code.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The guide says to check the root token and Vault server address if the message does not appear.

primary_path.action: Add code that reads the secret and extracts its password value.
details: Call client.KVv2("secret").Get for my-secret-password.
Fail on a read error.
Extract secret.Data["password"] as a string and fail if the type assertion is not valid.
primary_path.input: Stored secret path and authenticated client.
primary_path.output: Retrieved password value in the application.
primary_path.success_signal: The read and type assertion can complete without error.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A wrong path, failed request, or unexpected data type terminates the sample.

primary_path.action: Compare the retrieved value with the expected password and add the completion message.
details: Fail if the value is not Hashi123.
Print Access granted! when the value matches.
primary_path.output: Explicit value check and first-success signal.
primary_path.success_signal: The source contains the equality check and completion message.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An unexpected retrieved value terminates with an error.

primary_path.action: Run the completed program and observe the first-success result.
details: The same run writes the secret, reads it back, checks its value, and prints the final message.
primary_path.input: Completed Go program and running local Vault dev server.
primary_path.output: Verified first secret round trip through Vault.
primary_path.success_signal: Access granted! appears in terminal output.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If retrieval fails, the guide says to check that the secret path is correct; earlier address and token checks also still apply.

branches.condition: The developer uses a local Vault binary instead of Docker.
branches.path: Install Vault for the operating system, then run vault server -dev -dev-root-token-id="dev-only-token".
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes the Docker image and port-publication action but adds platform-specific Vault installation.

branches.condition: The developer selects Ruby, C#, Python, Java Spring, Bash/curl, or a beta OpenAPI client.
branches.path: Use the corresponding dependency, imports, client initialization, write, read, and verification tab throughout the guide.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The Vault server, token, secret path, value, and write/read milestone stay the same, while commands, code, and some output change.

branches.condition: The developer needs production-suitable application authentication.
branches.path: Choose and configure an appropriate auth method such as a Kubernetes or cloud-provider method rather than the development root token.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds auth-method configuration, identity, policy, and credential steps that the quick start defers to additional examples.

friction_gates.description: Docker or the Vault binary plus one supported language environment must be installed.
The developer must choose Docker versus local Vault and a supported implementation language.
Local port 8200 must be available and the Docker daemon must be able to run the Vault image.
The sample uses a full-access development root token that is explicitly unsafe for production.
The client address, token, KV mount, and secret path must match the running development server.

sources.title: Developer quick start
evidence_supported: Full Go setup, authentication, write, read, and verification journey

uncertainties.question: How many screens or package-manager prompts occur while installing Docker or a local Vault binary on a new workstation?
uncertainties.impact: The prerequisite installation can contain uncounted platform-specific actions before the documented application journey starts.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quick start links to external Docker installation or OS-specific Vault installation and does not define one workstation state.

uncertainties.question: Does Docker need to download the Vault image on the first run?
uncertainties.impact: An image download adds a network wait before the server starts.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: It depends on local Docker image state and the guide provides no deterministic wait.

excluded_after_success: Production-suitable authentication methods and least-privilege policies.
Dynamic database credentials and connection keepalive.
Cleanup of the local development server.
